summary: Uncle Bob responds to a blog post by a Ruby programmer who complained about an unfair whiteboard interview, arguing that the programmer's entitled attitude is more problematic than the interview itself. He criticizes the notion that employers should research candidates before meeting them and accommodate their preferred coding environment. Martin argues that companies shouldn't specifically seek Ruby programmers but rather experienced programmers who can learn any language quickly. The core message is that good developers should be able to extrapolate their skills to unfamiliar domains, and interviews should test this adaptability rather than specific language knowledge.
key_points: Interviews are supposed to be challenging - employers want candidates who stand out, not just anyone who might fit
Entitled attitudes from candidates are a red flag, regardless of market demand for their skills
Any competent programmer can learn Ruby and Rails within a month, so don't overpay for Ruby-specific expertise
The best interviews test a candidate's ability to extrapolate from their experience to unfamiliar problems
Technical specialists who can only work within their comfort zone are less valuable than adaptable generalists
money_quotes: The last thing I want is to hire someone who thinks I owe them a job.
You don't need Ruby Programmers! You just need programmers!
Any decent programmer can learn Ruby in a week. And Rails only takes a little longer.

Don't hire these prima donnas who think they're God's gift to Ruby shops. They're not. They're just generally inexperienced kids who happened to learn a pretty easy language and a pretty easy framework.
Find some programmers who have been around the block a few times. They should know multiple languages, and be able to deal with many different and novel situations.
What you are looking for are mexican chefs who don't panic when you ask them how to prepare crème brûlée.
You are looking to see whether or not he has the experience and resourcefulness to fruitfully attack the problem.
key_insight: A programmer's value lies not in mastery of a specific language but in their ability to adapt, learn quickly, and apply their experience to unfamiliar problems.
